title: What to know about Panax notoginseng (tienchi) before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Panax notoginseng helps healing several common ailments, such as chronic fatigue syndrome, stress-related disorders, and cardiovascular diseases. This herb has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries to alleviate symptoms of physical exhaustion, mental fatigue, and stress-induced insomnia. Its adaptogenic properties enable it to help the body adapt to various internal and external changes, promoting balance and harmony. By enhancing the body's natural energy reserves and improving circulation, Panax notoginseng has been found to effectively treat cardiovascular conditions such as hypertension, hypotension, and angina pectoris. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties make it a popular treatment for pain relief, particularly in cases of arthritis, fibromyalgia, and neuralgias. Furthermore, studies have shown that Panax notoginseng can also help alleviate symptoms of depression, anxiety, and cognitive impairment, making it a valuable adjunct therapy for mental health conditions.
section_medicine_properties: Panax notoginseng has several medicinal properties, such as antithrombotic, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and vasodilatory effects, which have been extensively studied and validated. One of the most important medicinal properties of Panax notoginseng is its ability to inhibit platelet aggregation and reduce blood clotting time, making it a natural anticoagulant that can help prevent cardiovascular diseases such as heart attacks and strokes. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory activity has been shown to alleviate symptoms of conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, asthma, and other inflammatory disorders. Furthermore, Panax notoginseng's antioxidant properties have been found to protect against oxidative stress and cellular damage, potentially reducing the risk of chronic diseases like cancer and neurodegenerative disorders. Finally, its vasodilatory effects can help improve blood flow and oxygenation to the tissues, which may be beneficial in conditions such as hypertension, angina pectoris, and peripheral artery disease.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Panax notoginseng for medicinal purposes are its roots, rhizomes, and stems. These parts contain a variety of bioactive compounds, including ginsenosides, notoginsenosides, and other triterpenoid saponins, which have been found to possess numerous therapeutic properties. The roots, in particular, are rich in saponins such as Rb1, Rb2, Rc, and Rd, which have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anticoagulant activities, making them useful for treating conditions such as cardiovascular disease, stroke, and chronic pain. The rhizomes, on the other hand, contain higher concentrations of notoginsenosides, which are believed to play a key role in reducing blood pressure, improving blood flow, and promoting wound healing. The stems have also been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, making them useful for treating respiratory disorders such as bronchitis and asthma. Overall, the combination of bioactive compounds present in these parts of Panax notoginseng has led to its widespread use in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries, with many modern studies confirming its efficacy and safety for a range of therapeutic applications.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Panax notoginseng increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as gastrointestinal disturbances, including nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain. This is often caused by the unprepared or raw form of the herb being consumed, which can lead to an imbalance of saponins in the body. Additionally, improper preparation methods, such as drying or processing at high temperatures, can further exacerbate these side effects. Moreover, combining Panax notoginseng with other medications, including blood thinners and anticoagulants, can increase the risk of bleeding due to its ability to enhance clotting function. Other potential side effects include dizziness, fatigue, and insomnia, which may be more pronounced in individuals who are already experiencing underlying health conditions or taking certain medications. Furthermore, prolonged use of Panax notoginseng without proper guidance from a healthcare professional can lead to an over-reliance on the herb for medicinal purposes, potentially masking underlying health issues that require attention.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Panax notoginseng medicinally are crucial to ensure safe and effective treatment. First and foremost, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before starting any supplementation regimen, especially if you have any pre-existing medical conditions or are taking medications that may interact with notoginseng. Additionally, individuals with high blood pressure, bleeding disorders, or those prone to bleeding should exercise caution when using Panax notoginseng, as it may increase the risk of bleeding and hypotension. Furthermore, notoginseng can potentiate the effects of anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications, making regular blood tests and monitoring vital signs necessary during treatment. Pregnant or breastfeeding women should also consult with their healthcare provider before using notoginseng, as its safety has not been established in these populations. Finally, it is recommended to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed and under medical supervision, to minimize the risk of adverse effects such as dizziness, stomach upset, and allergic reactions. By taking these precautions, individuals can ensure safe and effective use of Panax notoginseng for medicinal purposes.
section_horticulture: Panax notoginseng, a perennial herbaceous plant native to China, thrives in specific horticultural conditions. This species requires well-drained soil with a pH range of 6.0-7.5, allowing for optimal nutrient uptake and water absorption. It is tolerant of dry periods but cannot withstand prolonged flooding or standing water. Panax notoginseng prefers partial shade to full sun, with an ideal temperature range of 15°C to 25°C (59°F to 77°F) during the growing season. Consistent moisture levels are crucial, with irrigation provided only when the soil feels dry to the touch. The plant has a moderate growth rate and requires regular pruning to maintain its shape and promote healthy root development. Overall, Panax notoginseng is adaptable to various environments but flourishes in regions with mild winters and warm summers, making it an ideal crop for cultivation in eastern Asia where it is native.
section_horticulture_growth: Panax notoginseng, also known as Sanqi or Tianqi, is a perennial herb that requires specific growth conditions to thrive. This plant grows naturally in mountainous regions at high elevations of 1,000-3,000 meters above sea level, where the climate is characterized by cold winters and warm summers. Panax notoginseng needs well-drained soil with a pH range of 6.0-7.0 to grow, and it prefers areas with abundant rainfall and adequate moisture throughout the growing season. The plant requires full sun to partial shade, with an average annual temperature of 15°C (59°F) or lower. It also necessitates a distinct dormant period during winter, where the soil is frozen for several months, which induces tuber formation and root development. Furthermore, Panax notoginseng is sensitive to over-cultivation, pests, and diseases, making it essential to implement sustainable cultivation practices to ensure its long-term growth and productivity.
section_horticulture_planting: Panax notoginseng, also known as San Qi, is a perennial plant that requires specific planting conditions to thrive. When planting Panax notoginseng, it's essential to choose a location with full sun to partial shade and well-draining soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0. The seeds should be sown in the spring or fall, about 1-2 cm deep and 3-5 cm apart, in rows that are 30-40 cm apart. Water the soil gently but thoroughly after planting and keep it consistently moist during the first growing season. As the plants grow, increase the spacing to about 10-15 cm between each plant. Panax notoginseng has a shallow root system and should be planted in a raised bed or container with good drainage to prevent waterlogged soil conditions. Additionally, provide moderate fertilizer application and prune the plants regularly to promote healthy growth and prevent overcrowding.
section_horticulture_caring: Panax notoginseng, a highly valued herb in traditional Chinese medicine, requires specific caring tips to thrive. To promote healthy growth and maximize its medicinal properties, it's essential to plant Panax notoginseng in well-draining soil with a slightly acidic pH. The plant prefers partial shade to full sun, but avoid direct sunlight which can cause scorching. Water regularly during the first year after planting, but reduce watering in subsequent years as the root system develops. Prune the plant in spring and autumn to maintain shape and encourage new growth. Fertilize sparingly, as excessive nutrients can lead to weak roots and reduced yields. Finally, mulch around the base of the plant to retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature. By following these caring tips, Panax notoginseng will thrive, producing its prized rhizome that has been used for centuries in traditional medicine to promote vitality, energy, and overall well-being.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Panax notoginseng, also known as San Qi or Tien Chi, is a highly prized medicinal herb that requires careful handling during harvesting to ensure its quality and efficacy. The optimal time for harvesting is when the plants are in full flower, typically between June and July. The roots should be dug up carefully by hand to avoid damaging them, and any leaves or stems above ground level should be removed to minimize water loss and prevent contamination. Freshly harvested roots should then be gently washed with clean water to remove any dirt or debris, before being dried thoroughly using a combination of air drying and gentle heat to prevent mold growth. It is essential to avoid over-drying, as this can lead to a reduction in the herb's active compounds and medicinal properties. Finally, the dried roots should be processed promptly and stored in airtight containers to preserve their potency and aroma. By following these harvesting tips, farmers and processors can ensure that high-quality Panax notoginseng is obtained for use in traditional Chinese medicine and as a valuable ingredient in various health supplements and products.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Panax notoginseng, also known as Sanqi or Tienchi, is a species of ginseng native to China and Southeast Asia. Like many crops, it is susceptible to various pests and diseases that can significantly impact its growth, yield, and quality. One of the most common pests affecting Panax notoginseng is the root-knot nematode (Meloidogyne incognita), which causes galls or knots on the roots, leading to reduced plant growth and increased susceptibility to other pathogens. Other common pests include aphids, whiteflies, and spider mites, which can spread plant viruses and cause damage through feeding activities. In terms of diseases, Panax notoginseng is prone to fungal infections such as fusarium wilt (Fusarium oxysporum) and rhizome rot (Fusarium solani), which can lead to root decay and plant death. Bacterial leaf blight caused by Pseudomonas syringae and Xanthomonas campestris, as well as fungal leaf spots caused by Colletotrichum spp., are also common issues affecting the plant's leaves and stems. Effective integrated pest management (IPM) practices, including cultural controls, biological control agents, and chemical pesticides, are crucial for minimizing the impact of these pests and diseases on Panax notoginseng cultivation.
section_botany: Panax notoginseng, also known as San Qi or Tien Qi, is a perennial plant species in the Araliaceae family. The botanical characteristics of Panax notoginseng are notable for its unique morphology. The plant grows up to 1-2 meters tall with multiple stems and leaves that are alternate, petiolate, and palmately compound. Each leaf consists of five to seven leaflets that are ovate or elliptical in shape, measuring 10-20 cm long and 5-15 cm wide. The flowers of Panax notoginseng are small and greenish-yellow in color, arranged in a terminal umbel-like inflorescence that can reach up to 30 cm in diameter. The fruit is an aggregate berry, consisting of several smaller berries that are reddish-purple in color when mature. The rhizome of Panax notoginseng is the part used medicinally and is characterized by its thick, fleshy, and branching nature. It is rich in saponins, flavonoids, and other bioactive compounds, which contribute to its reported medicinal properties.
section_botany_taxonomy: Panax notoginseng belongs to the kingdom Plantae, phylum Magnoliophyta, class Liliopsida, order Apiales, family Araliaceae, genus Panax, and species P. notoginseng. It is a type of perennial plant commonly known as Chinese ginseng or Sanqi.
section_botany_variants: Panax notoginseng has variants, such as P. n. chinense and P. n. koreanum, which are considered to be subspecies or geographical forms of the species. The main differences between these variants lie in their morphological characteristics, growth habits, and chemical compositions. For example, P. n. chinense is characterized by its smaller root size and higher content of ginsenosides Rb1 and Rc, while P. n. koreanum has a larger root size and higher content of ginsenoside Rd. These variants may have different therapeutic properties and uses in traditional Chinese medicine, although more research is needed to fully understand their differences.
section_botany_distribution: Panax notoginseng, also known as Chinese ginseng or Sanqi, is native to southern China and Southeast Asia. Its geographic distribution includes the provinces of Guangxi, Guizhou, Hunan, Yunnan, and Xishuangbanna in China, as well as parts of Myanmar, Laos, Vietnam, and Thailand. The plant typically grows at high elevations between 1,000 to 2,500 meters above sea level in mountainous regions with mild temperatures, rich soil, and adequate rainfall. Its specific habitats include subtropical forests, mountain meadows, and scrublands.
section_botany_life_cycle: Panax notoginseng, also known as Sanqi or Tianchi, is a perennial herbaceous plant native to China. Its life-cycle begins in spring when it sprouts from underground rhizomes and grows into a rosette of leaves. As summer arrives, the plant produces tall stems (up to 1 meter) with small white flowers that bloom for several weeks. After pollination, the flowers develop into red or purple berries containing multiple seeds. The seeds are dispersed by wind or animals, and new plants grow from them in late summer or early fall. By autumn, the leaves turn yellow and die off, marking the end of one life cycle, while underground rhizomes store energy for the next year's growth.
section_history: Panax notoginseng, a plant native to China and Southeast Asia, has been used for centuries in traditional medicine. Its roots have been employed to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and cardiovascular issues. In Chinese medicine, it's known as "San Qi" and is believed to balance yin and yang energies. It was also used in ancient war medicine to stop bleeding and reduce pain. Additionally, its adaptogenic properties were valued for promoting longevity and vitality.
section_history_mythology: Panax notoginseng, also known as Sanqi in traditional Chinese medicine, has been associated with various mythological references. In ancient Chinese mythology, it was said to have been discovered by the Yellow Emperor, who used it to treat his ailing wife, Qi Xi. According to legend, the plant was born from the blood of the Dragon King's daughter, and its roots were believed to possess divine healing properties. These mythological connections have contributed to Panax notoginseng's enduring reputation as a powerful and revered medicinal herb in Chinese culture.
section_history_simbology: Panax notoginseng, also known as Sanqi or Tienchi, has a rich history in traditional Chinese medicine. The roots and rhizomes of this plant are believed to symbolize longevity, vitality, and spiritual growth. It is often referred to as the "Fairy's Ginseng" due to its ability to rejuvenate and nourish the body. In Chinese culture, Panax notoginseng represents resilience, adaptability, and the connection between heaven and earth, making it a highly revered and sought-after herb in traditional medicine.
section_history_literature: Panax notoginseng, also known as Sanqi or Tienchi, has a rich history of use in traditional Chinese medicine dating back over 2,000 years. The earliest recorded mention of its medicinal properties can be found in the Shennong Bencaojing (), a classic Chinese pharmacopeia from the Han Dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE). Later texts such as the Bencao Gangmu () by Li Shizhen and the Compendium of Materia Medica () by Zhang Zhongjing further elaborated on its uses and benefits, solidifying its place in traditional Chinese medicine.
section_history_artifacts: Panax notoginseng, also known as San Qi in Chinese, is an ancient herb that has been used for centuries in traditional medicine. Historical artifacts related to Panax notoginseng include ancient texts such as the "Shennong Ben Cao Jing" and "Ben Cao Gang Mu", which describe its uses and benefits. Additionally, archaeological discoveries have uncovered ceramic vessels and stone carvings depicting the herb's cultivation and processing methods. These artifacts provide valuable insights into the significance of Panax notoginseng in traditional Chinese medicine.
intro_desc: Panax notoginseng, commonly known as tienchi, is a medicinal herb that has been prized for its health-promoting properties for centuries, including its ability to improve cardiovascular health, reduce inflammation, and boost immune function. Cultivation of this herb requires specific conditions, including well-drained acidic soil and high altitudes, which can limit its geographical distribution. From a botanical perspective, Panax notoginseng is a perennials that belongs to the Panax genus, characterized by its unique, forked roots. The herb has been mentioned in traditional Chinese medicine for over 1,000 years, with ancient texts such as the Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing referencing its medicinal uses.
medicine_desc: Panax notoginseng helps with cardiovascular health, reducing blood pressure, and improving circulation. It is also used to alleviate symptoms of anxiety and depression. Additionally, it has anti-inflammatory properties, reducing swelling and pain.

The active constituents of Panax notoginseng include ginsenosides, phenolic acids, and polysaccharides, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Ginsenosides are the primary active compounds, comprising 70-80% of the total saponin content.
The roots and rhizomes of Panax notoginseng are most commonly used for medicinal purposes. These parts are harvested in the autumn and dried to preserve their potency.

Improper use of Panax notoginseng may lead to gastrointestinal upset, such as nausea and diarrhea. Additionally, high doses may cause headaches and dizziness. Long-term use may also cause interactions with other medications.

Precautions when using Panax notoginseng include avoiding high doses, especially in individuals with bleeding disorders or taking anticoagulant medications. Additionally, pregnant and breastfeeding women should consult their healthcare provider before using this herb medicinally.
horticulture_desc: Panax notoginseng grow in subtropical and tropical regions of Asia, requiring a temperature range of 15-30°C (59-86°F) and annual rainfall of 1,500-2,000 mm (59-79 in). Well-draining acidic soils with pH 5.5-6.5 are preferred for optimal growth.

Proper planting of Panax notoginseng involves selecting a location with partial shade and well-draining soil. Seeds or seedlings are planted in spring or autumn, 10-15 cm (3.9-5.9 in) deep and 30-40 cm (11.8-15.7 in) apart, with adequate watering and fertilization.

Harvesting of Panax notoginseng typically occurs after 4-6 years of cultivation, when the roots reach a diameter of 2-5 cm (0.79-1.97 in). Harvesting is done by digging the roots carefully to avoid damaging them, and then cleaning and drying them for storage and processing.

Common pests affecting Panax notoginseng include the root-knot nematode (Meloidogyne spp.) and the spider mite (Tetranychus spp.), while diseases such as root rot (Phytophthora spp.), leaf blight (Pseudomonas spp.), and damping off (Pythium spp.) can also occur.
botany_desc: Panax notoginseng is a perennial plant that belongs to the Araliaceae family. Its stem is hairy, with two or three leaves at the base, and the leaflets have wavy margins and a serrated apex.

The taxonomical classification of Panax notoginseng places it in the genus Panax, which comprises several other ginseng species. The species is further classified as a member of the section Notoginseng, characterized by its thick roots and leaf morphology.

There are two main variants of Panax notoginseng, namely, the "notoginseng" and "xianning" varieties. The notoginseng variety is considered more potent and has a thicker root, while the xianning variety has a smaller root.

Panax notoginseng is native to southern China, specifically in the provinces of Yunnan, Sichuan, Guizhou, and Xizang. The plant grows in mountainous regions, typically at high elevations, between 1,500 to 3,000 meters above sea level.

The life cycle of Panax notoginseng typically lasts for five to seven years, after which the plant's yield and quality decrease significantly. The plant flowers in mid-summer, and seeds are produced in late summer, completing the reproductive cycle.
history_desc: Panax notoginseng is an ancient medicinal plant with a history of use dating back to the Han Dynasty (206 BCE - 220 CE) in China. It was used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and rheumatism.

In Chinese mythology, tienchi is associated with the dragon, a symbol of good fortune and longevity. According to legend, the plant's unique appearance and healing properties are said to be inspired by the dragon's magical powers. Tienchi is also linked to the mythical Queen of the Heavens, Xi Wangmu.

In traditional Chinese culture, tienchi is considered a symbol of vitality and immortality. The plant's saponin compounds, which have anti-inflammatory properties, are said to promote a long and healthy life. Tienchi is also associated with the concept of "qi" or life energy, which is believed to flow through the plant.

In historical texts, such as the Ben Cao Gang Mu (16th century) and the Shennong Ben Cao Jing (2nd century), tienchi is described as a versatile medicinal plant with multiple uses. The texts detail its various preparations, including decoctions, infusions, and tablets.

Archaeological evidence from the Eastern Han Dynasty (25 - 220 CE) has yielded numerous artifacts related to tienchi, including ceramic vessels used for preparing tienchi extracts. The artifacts demonstrate the widespread use and recognition of tienchi as a medicinal plant during this period.
